segments.text: On three different continents. In four different decades. On a drug whose entire experience lasts seven minutes. Strangers describe the same thing — vertical glyphs of light, kanji-like, scrolling top to bottom. A writing system the brain recognizes as language without being able to translate.

And when practitioners cross two laser beams in front of their closed eyes during the peak — one violet, one green — the glyphs reportedly sharpen. Get crisper. Some people use the word legible. The community has a name for it. They call it the Matrix code.

Johns Hopkins surveyed two thousand five hundred sixty one of those strangers in 2020. Imperial College London mapped their brains in 2019. So this isn't fringe pseudoscience anymore. There's a peer-reviewed paper trail. And the question we want to walk through today is — why are unrelated people reporting the same thing.
Right. And this is where I want to set the frame. Because there are three audiences walking into this question, and they each hear it differently.
Walk me through them.

First — the person who watched The Matrix and never quite got over the question Morpheus asked. The simulation question. Whether reality has another layer. The DMT laser corpus is, weirdly, the closest thing we have to a witness pool for that question.

Second — the person who suspects there are things they aren't being told. The Epstein file, MK-Ultra, Stargate, that whole shape of inquiry. Because what we keep finding in this archive is a pattern. Peer-reviewed evidence that doesn't move the institutions tasked with handling it. The DMT laser corpus has the same shape.

And third — the biohacker. The person stacking cold plunge and breathwork and sleep architecture, who's been reading Wim Hof and Joe Dispenza, and who notices the wellness world keeps quietly pointing at this molecule. Imperial College's data shows the DMT brain state is electrophysiologically close to REM sleep. Which is the state biohackers have been chasing through breath-holds for years.
OK. Three doors. Same archive. Let's open the first one. Tell me what the protocol actually is — because I want to be clear that this is not what Strassman did at UNM in the nineties.

Right. The laser protocol is not academic. It does not appear in Rick Strassman's UNM trials. It does not appear in the Imperial College extended-state DMT work. It exists primarily in the self-experiment literature — Reddit, Erowid, podcast interviews with people who decline to be named.
So how do they describe the setup?

Two narrow-beam laser pointers. Typically one violet at four hundred five nanometers, one green at five hundred thirty two nanometers. Crossed at eye level, several feet in front of a reclining subject. Eyes closed. DMT vaporized at threshold dose, thirty five to forty five milligrams. Onset is thirty seconds. Peak is around the ninety to one hundred twenty second mark.
And during that peak, what they're describing is — the brain is already generating geometric visual content under closed eyes. The lasers add a structured input on top of it.

The claim is exactly that. They're not saying the lasers cause the visions. They're saying the lasers stabilize what was already there. Which is a much smaller, much more testable claim. The structures snap onto the lattice the lasers describe. Glyphs become readable. Almost always scrolling vertically. Top to bottom.
Same direction as the green code in The Matrix.

Same direction as the green code in The Matrix. Which Lana and Lilly Wachowski wrote in 1999 — based, by their own account, on a sushi cookbook. Green text, black background, falling vertically. They were not, to anyone's documented knowledge, reading DMT trip reports. And yet the falling-glyph form is what closed-eye DMT subjects had already been describing for years before that film came out.
OK that is genuinely strange.

It is. And it leads directly to the next question — why kanji. Why not Cyrillic? Why not Mayan? Why not Linear B? The reports almost universally reach for Japanese-script-like as the comparison. That is a specific shape of convergence.
Let's take the most parsimonious explanation first. The one that doesn't require entities, transmissions, hyperspace. The visual cortex hypothesis.

Right. So in 1928, Heinrich Klüver — German psychologist — documented four recurring geometric forms. Lattices. Cobwebs. Tunnels. Spirals. They appeared in mescaline subjects regardless of culture. He called them form constants.

And then in 2001, Bressloff, Cowan, and colleagues showed mathematically that those four forms map directly onto the geometry of primary visual cortex — V one. The cortex has a hexagonal-like neural lattice. Under serotonergic disruption, it projects itself onto perception.

Which means a crossed-laser stimulus might be doing something simple. Seeding the visual field with a coherent structured input. The cortex is in a high-entropy state from DMT's 5-HT2A agonism. It uses the input as scaffolding. The hallucinated glyphs snap onto the lattice the lasers describe.
In which case every subject is seeing the same thing — not because they're seeing the same place — but because they have the same cortex.

That is the most defensible scientific reading of the data. And I want to be clear it does the heavy lifting on the geometry. What it does not yet do is account for the felt sense of meaning. The conviction subjects report that the code is legible. That it's for them. That it's being transmitted. That part is harder.
Let's bring up the obvious counter. Confirmation bias. People who post on Reddit have read Reddit. The corpus is contaminated.

True. And honest. But three checks survive that critique. The Johns Hopkins survey constrained respondents into pre-defined phenomenology categories rather than letting them free-write. The convergence held under that structure. Lawrence and team in 2022 did a quantitative content analysis at the University of Greenwich — entity contact and geometric language clustered statistically, not just narratively.
And the third check?

The historical record. Strassman's UNM subjects in the early nineties — pre-internet — could not have read each other's reports. They were screened. Transcribed within minutes of dosing. And a subset of them described entity contact and language-like communication.

So confirmation bias accounts for some. But not for the historical residual. And not for the statistical residual once you constrain the categories. There's something there that the obvious skeptical move can't dissolve.

Which is exactly why we file this in the phenomena pillar. Not in believer territory. Not in skeptic territory. The convergence is documentable. The mechanism is partly understood. The content of the convergence — why kanji, why scrolling, why a felt sense of being addressed — sits at the edge of what current neuroscience can describe.
Last question. If a listener hears this and the question opens, what is your closing frame for them.

The data that doesn't fit the story doesn't disappear. It accumulates. The investigator's job is to notice the accumulation, hold the mechanism and the mystery in the same hand, and refuse to file either one early. That's the whole project. That's what we do here.
Subscribe at the bottom of the page. One transmission a week. The rest of the code is in there.
